title: Quiz: Quantum Reservoir Computing Basics
description: Test your understanding of quantum reservoir computing fundamentals

questions.question: What replaces the classical neural network in quantum reservoir computing?

options.text: A quantum system evolving under its Hamiltonian

questions.explanation: In QRC, the reservoir is a quantum system that evolves under its natural Hamiltonian dynamics. This evolution provides the nonlinear, high-dimensional transformation of inputs.

questions.question: How are features extracted from a quantum reservoir?

options.text: By reading the quantum state directly
By measuring expectation values of observables

questions.explanation: Features are extracted by measuring expectation values ⟨O⟩ = Tr(ρO) of chosen observables (typically Pauli operators). This is necessary because quantum states cannot be read directly.

questions.question: For N qubits, what is the dimension of the quantum reservoir's Hilbert space?
questions.explanation: An N-qubit system has a 2^N dimensional Hilbert space, providing exponentially more dimensions than a classical reservoir with N neurons.

questions.question: What quantum resource can enhance the computational power of quantum reservoirs?
questions.explanation: Entanglement creates quantum correlations between qubits that have no classical analog. These correlations can enable richer feature representations and enhance computational capabilities.

questions.question: In quantum reservoir computing, the readout layer is trained using quantum algorithms.
questions.explanation: False. Just like classical reservoir computing, only the readout layer is trained, and it uses classical ridge regression. The quantum part is the reservoir dynamics, not the training.

questions.question: What physical effect limits the memory in real quantum reservoirs?
questions.explanation: Decoherence causes quantum coherence to decay over time, effectively limiting how long quantum information can be stored. This creates a trade-off between evolution time and information preservation.

questions.question: How is classical input typically encoded into a quantum reservoir?

options.text: As binary strings in computational basis
As rotation angles in parameterized gates

questions.explanation: Classical inputs are typically encoded as rotation angles (e.g., Ry(u·π)) applied to input qubits. This creates superposition states whose amplitudes depend on the input values.

questions.question: Which are potential advantages of quantum reservoirs over classical ones? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Quantum reservoirs offer (a) 2^N dimensional Hilbert space, (b) entanglement-based correlations, and (d) measurement-induced nonlinearity. Training speed is similar since both use linear regression.
